title: Hive
objectives: Understand Hive via a real-life analogy
Distinguish it from SQLite / Isar / SharedPreferences

sections.markdown:
**Think of Hive as a set of labeled shoeboxes on a shelf.**

- Each **box** is a named collection of key-value pairs. You open a box (`Hive.openBox('settings')`), write things into it (`box.put('theme', 'dark')`), read them (`box.get('theme')`), close it. That's it.
- No SQL. No schema. No JOINs. Just 'put a value in a box by a key; get it back later by the same key'. Same mental model as a `Map<String, dynamic>` — except it persists to disk.
- **You don't ask 'find all users where age > 18'.** You iterate the box or use pre-organized keys. It's a KV store, not a query engine.

**In code:** Hive is a pure-Dart, NoSQL, key-value database designed for Flutter. **Zero native code** — pure Dart means it works on every Flutter platform (mobile, web, desktop, embedded) without platform channels. Zero configuration. Very fast for its use case (~10x faster than SQLite for pure KV lookups).

**Where it shines:**
- **App settings** — theme, locale, feature flags.
- **Tokens + session state** — auth token, refresh token, user profile.
- **Small caches** — 'last 20 items I showed the user'.
- **Progress / bookmarks** — one row per user; simple KV.
- **Any 'give me this specific thing by ID' workload.**

**Where it doesn't:**
- Any query beyond by-key — 'orders where total > 100'? Hive can't. You'd have to iterate every row.
- Relational data with joins — no such concept.
- Large datasets where you need query engine performance — use SQLite/Isar/Drift.

**The truth about Hive in 2026:** Hive itself is in **maintenance mode** since ~2022. The original author moved to **Isar** (see next topic) which shares the design goals but is more powerful. **Hive Community Edition (Hive CE)** is a community fork continuing maintenance. For new projects consider Isar; existing Hive codebases can keep going indefinitely — the library is stable and the use case (KV persistence) doesn't need active development.

**Hive** is a lightweight, pure-Dart NoSQL key-value database designed for Flutter. Data lives in **boxes** (like maps). Zero native code — works on all Flutter platforms. Very fast for KV workloads. Supports custom types via **TypeAdapters** (generated by build_runner). Optional AES encryption per box. Best for app settings, tokens, small caches, progress tracking. Not for complex queries or relational data (use Drift/Isar). **Hive Community Edition (Hive CE)** is the actively-maintained fork as of 2026.
## The core API — 90% of what you'll use
## Box types — pick the right one

rows: Loads ALL entries into RAM on open
Values fetched from disk on demand
10k+ entries or big values; slower reads

sections.markdown: **Rule of thumb:** if your box will hold more than a few thousand items OR entries with big payloads (>1KB each), use `LazyBox`. Otherwise regular `Box` is faster (in-memory access after first load).
## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: Reaching for Hive for query-heavy workloads.** 'Find orders where total > 100' → Hive iterates every entry. Use SQLite (drift) or Isar.
**Mistake 2: Storing the encryption key in the app.** Anyone with the APK can extract it. Store in Secure Storage (`flutter_secure_storage` — Keychain on iOS, Keystore on Android).
**Mistake 3: Forgetting to close boxes on app termination.** Hive handles most cases, but explicitly `await Hive.close()` on shutdown ensures pending writes flush.

**Mistake 4: Regenerating TypeAdapters and forgetting a version bump.** Changing `@HiveField` numbers or types on existing data → data corruption. Add new fields with NEW field numbers; never reuse or reorder.
**Mistake 5: Using `Box` for 100k entries.** Loads everything into RAM. Use `LazyBox` for large datasets.
**Mistake 6: Storing binary blobs > 1MB.** Slow; better filesystem + path in Hive.
## Best practices
**1. Use Hive for KV workloads only.** Settings, tokens, small caches.
**2. Encryption key in Secure Storage.** Never in code, config, or plain Hive.
**3. Multiple small boxes over one giant box.** Better organization + smaller RAM footprint.
**4. `@HiveField` numbers are permanent.** Never reuse or renumber; add new fields with new numbers.
**5. `LazyBox` for large or big-value collections.** Frees RAM.
**6. `box.watch()` for reactive UI.** Perfect Flutter integration.
**7. Test migrations.** Old data + new adapter version = one of Flutter's most-common crashes. Test it.
**8. Consider Isar for new projects in 2026.** Same author; more capable; still zero-config.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Theme + locale.** Persist theme + locale settings across restarts.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — TypeAdapter for User.** Store User objects in a typed box.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Reactive UI.** `ValueListenableBuilder<Box>` or `box.watch()` → rebuild widget on change.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Encrypted box.** Store auth token encrypted; key in Secure Storage.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — Schema evolution.** Add a new field to User; bump adapter; test backward compat.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — Migrate Hive → Isar.** Take a Hive-heavy app, port to Isar; keep a compatibility layer to migrate existing user data.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Hive and when do you use it?**

**A:** Hive is a lightweight, pure-Dart, NoSQL key-value database for Flutter. **Pure-Dart** means it works on every Flutter platform without native plugins — huge for cross-platform. Data lives in **boxes** — named collections of key-value pairs (like `Map<String, dynamic>` that persists). Very fast for KV workloads. **Use for:** settings, feature flags, auth tokens, small caches, per-user progress. **Don't use for:** query-heavy workloads (Hive iterates), relational data, big data. **In 2026:** the original author moved to Isar, so consider Isar for new projects. Existing Hive apps are fine to keep; Hive CE (Community Edition) continues maintenance.

**Q2: Hive vs SharedPreferences?**

**A:** Both persist simple KV. **SharedPreferences** — thin wrapper over platform-native storage (iOS `NSUserDefaults`, Android `SharedPreferences`). Simple. Best for a handful of small values. Limited to primitive types (String, int, bool, double, List<String>). Synchronous read after first load. **Hive** — full DB. Supports typed complex objects via TypeAdapters. Supports encryption. Reactive `.watch()` streams. Multiple boxes for organization. Faster for many operations. **Rule:** 3-5 flags/values → SharedPreferences. Anything more complex → Hive (or Isar). Both are fine choices; Hive gives you more room to grow.

**Q4: How do you encrypt a Hive box?**

**A:** Use `HiveAesCipher` when opening the box:
```dart
await Hive.openBox('sensitive', encryptionCipher: HiveAesCipher(key));
```
The key must be 32 bytes (256-bit AES). **The critical part is key STORAGE.** Never store the key in code (decompilable) or in the Hive DB itself (defeats the purpose). Store it in **`flutter_secure_storage`** — Keychain on iOS, Keystore on Android. First-launch pattern: generate key with `Hive.generateSecureKey()`, save to Secure Storage; subsequent launches read from Secure Storage. **Key rotation:** open old box with old key, copy to new box with new key, delete old box, update Secure Storage. Encryption is straightforward; key management is the hard part.

**Q5: What's the difference between Box and LazyBox?**

**A:** **`Box`** — when you open it, Hive loads ALL entries into RAM. Subsequent reads are near-instant (memory access). Downside: RAM usage grows with box size. **`LazyBox`** — entries fetched from disk on demand. Reads slightly slower (disk); RAM stays low. **Rule of thumb:** `Box` for <10k entries with small values (settings, tokens, current user). `LazyBox` for many entries or big values (5MB+ per entry). API is nearly identical (`get`, `put`, `delete`, `keys`); LazyBox's `.get()` returns `Future<T?>` instead of `T?`. Migration between them is straightforward when needed.

**Q6: Hive vs Drift/Isar for a Flutter local DB?**

**A:** **Hive** — KV only. No queries beyond by-key. Pure Dart. Fastest for pure KV. Best for settings, tokens, small caches. **Drift** — full SQLite with typed Dart schema + reactive streams. Best for relational data, JOINs, complex queries, offline-first apps with real data models. **Isar** — modern NoSQL with query DSL, indexes, reactive streams. Fast. Best if you want NoSQL flexibility + queries + speed. **Rule for 2026:** small KV needs → Hive (or SharedPreferences). Real data + relational + queries → Drift. Real data + NoSQL + queries + performance → Isar. Match tool to the workload; don't force one everywhere. Many apps use Hive for auth + Drift for real data — a fine architecture.

**Q7: What are Hive's limitations?**

**A:** **(1) No queries beyond by-key.** Filter / aggregate → iterate manually. **(2) No JOINs, no relations.** Model everything as denormalized single documents or use a real DB. **(3) `Box` loads everything into RAM.** Use `LazyBox` for large collections. **(4) TypeAdapter schema is fragile.** Reusing field numbers = data corruption. **(5) No native SQL** — one more thing to learn instead of leveraging SQL knowledge. **(6) Hive itself is in maintenance mode** — the community-fork Hive CE is the active continuation; the original author moved to Isar. For any of these limitations, either accept them (KV is the right tool) or move to Drift/Isar. Rule: Hive is CORRECT for a specific KV niche; wrong for anything relational.

## Real-world usage

- **Countless Flutter apps** for settings, tokens, small caches — likely the most-used Flutter local DB by app count.
- **This SE Learning Platform** uses Hive for progress / XP tracking.
- **Chat apps** — cached message previews, unread counts.
- **Media apps** — 'recently played', playback position per track.
- **Test suites** — quick persistent state without SQLite ceremony.
- **NOT for** — analytics apps with complex queries, offline-first apps with real relational data.

## Advanced notes

- **Hive CE (Community Edition)** — the maintained fork.
- **`HiveObject`** — legacy pattern; prefer plain classes + TypeAdapters.
- **`box.putAll({...})`** — batch write; faster than individual puts.
- **`box.compact()`** — reclaim space from deleted entries.
- **`Hive.close()`** — closes all boxes; call on app teardown.
- **`Hive.deleteFromDisk()`** — delete the whole box file.
- **`Hive.initFlutter()`** vs **`Hive.init(path)`** — Flutter uses the app document directory automatically; Dart CLI apps need an explicit path.
- **Multiple isolates + Hive** — each isolate opens its own instance; no cross-isolate sharing.
- **File location** — `getApplicationDocumentsDirectory()` on mobile; browser IndexedDB on web.
- **Backup** — copy the `.hive` and `.lock` files.
- **Testing** — `Hive.init('./test-hive')` + `Hive.deleteFromDisk` between tests.
- **Migrations** — no schema versioning built in; handle in your init code (open box, check version key, transform).

flashcards.back: Pure-Dart KV database for Flutter. Zero native code. Fast for KV; no queries.
SharedPreferences: 3-5 primitive values. Hive: complex objects, encryption, watching, multiple boxes.
@HiveField numbers are PERMANENT. Never reuse or reorder. Add new fields with new numbers.
flutter_secure_storage (Keychain/Keystore). NEVER in code or the Hive DB itself.
Box loads everything into RAM. LazyBox fetches on demand. LazyBox for >10k entries or big values.
Hive is in maintenance; Isar is the successor. Existing apps fine. Hive CE is the active fork.

options: Not supported — iterate manually or use SQL DB
PERMANENT — never reuse; add new fields with new numbers

challenges.text: TypeAdapter for a User class; add + read + update.
Reactive UI: ValueListenableBuilder<Box> refreshes on any change.
Encrypted 'session' box; key in Secure Storage; rotate key without data loss.
Schema evolution: add new field to User; verify old data reads correctly.
Migrate an app from Hive to Isar with a compat layer for existing user data.
